title: Today's Tech Brief: How some data center operators are tackling their water use problems
summary: A look at data center water use and Google's shift away from Gemini CLI
imageAlt: Image from source article for How some data center operators are tackling their water use problems
imageCaption: How some data center operators are tackling their water use problems
imageSourceTitle: How some data center operators are tackling their water use problems

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your tech brief. Let's dive into some fresh news. First up, have you ever stopped to think about how much water your favorite online services use? I'm not just talking about your virtual water footprint from streaming videos all day. Data centers, the backbone of our digital world, guzzle a surprising amount of water to keep things cool. Some operators are now trying to tackle that problem head-on.

According to a recent report from Ars Technica, some data center operators are exploring new ways to reduce their water usage. This matters because, as our reliance on cloud services grows, so does the strain on local water resources. I'm looking at you, AI and machine learning workloads. It's a complex issue, but it's great to see operators taking steps to address it. For instance, some companies are turning to innovative cooling methods, like using seawater or even experimenting with immersion cooling. It's fascinating to see how the industry is adapting to these challenges.

In other news, Google has made a move that's left some developers scratching their heads. The company has effectively sunset its Gemini CLI, a popular tool for interacting with its AI models. What's interesting here is that Google is nudging users toward a closed-source AI solution called 'Antigravity.' My read is that this shift could have implications for the future of AI development and accessibility. It's a bit of a bummer for those who relied on Gemini CLI, but it might also signal a new era for AI innovation.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
